title: NYC's Grassroots AI Safety Movement: Come As You Are
author: Elizabeth Safran
content:
I've been attending NYC AI events for a while, but lately, I've veered away from events for builders and entrepreneurs to more civic-minded, grassroots events. While less highbrow, the grassroots community is way more earnest and welcoming. Plus the AI safety movement in general is having a moment. The community is flourishing, and bearing witness to a burgeoning community is a beautiful thing.

To preserve that beauty, I'm doubling down on grassroots AI Safety.

It's not that the enterprise tech events weren't interesting or worthwhile. But for the most part, underpinning the enthusiasm for AI was a faint, almost religious zeal, oftentimes tinged with greed. What bothered me about these events was the absence of any conversation about AI security. One organizer dismissed my concerns and told me, "It's just math."

As if math has never caused harm. Tell that to people who lived through the 2008 financial crisis, when a few fancy equations created by greedy true believer bankers helped unravel the global economy. Sound familiar?

To be fair, these were AI events, not cybersecurity events, and many of them had a crypto slant. And crypto bros aren't exactly known for being risk-averse. Even so, whenever I'd try to veer the discussion towards the cyber risks associated with AI, I was tolerated at best as an amusing Cassandra, and at worst, barely tolerated. But mostly, folks were politely dismissive. No offense to me, they just didn't give a shit.

At one AI meetup, a guy from a top-3 bank -- an AI product manager -- casually mentioned during the Q&A after his presentation that shadow AI was commonplace at his workplace. His claim was backed up by another presenter, an AI infrastructure vendor who sold to his bank and other large companies in highly regulated industries. Companies whose workers *should* know better. This is old news for security folks but clearly, we need more than technical controls. We need a culture shift.

Security leaders often talk about security blind spots caused by siloed tech stacks, but when it comes to shadow AI, our human tech stacks are siloed. AI security experts are outnumbered by AI acolytes who see security as a problem that AI will solve. Just like it did(n't) for [Replit](https://fortune.com/2025/07/23/ai-coding-tool-replit-wiped-database-called-it-a-catastrophic-failure/) and [OpenClaw](https://elizabethsafran888347.substack.com/p/if-chatgpt-is-ai-cocaine-openclaw). Sometimes I think tech folks get so drunk out on their own Kool-Aid that they stop making sense. Or lose their common sense. Not unlike our AI overlords, but on a smaller scale.

Furthermore, from on-prem to the cloud and the cloud to AI, when it comes to cybersecurity, business leaders appear to be repeating the same mistakes yet again. And you know what they say: When people don't learn from their mistakes, they're doomed to repeat them.

Which leads me to why I'm going grassroots. My prime motivation isn't the existential risk (at least not yet), plus, there's a lot to like about AI. It helps me write better content faster and I believe in its potential to turbocharge progress in aspects of healthcare and climate safety.

I'm more worried about its shorter-term risks. With [20% of all layoffs in 2026](https://technode.global/2026/03/09/2026-tech-layoffs-reach-45000-in-march-more-than-9200-due-to-ai-and-automation-rationalfx/) attributed to AI (and I'm sure that number is low), I think it's fair to say the [massive economic disruption](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/01/27/dario-amodei-warns-ai-cause-unusually-painful-disruption-jobs.html) frontier lab CEOs keep talking about is well on its way. The legions of newly unemployed will join an already lonely, isolated populace polarized by social media. At the rate we're going, it won't be an amoral, misaligned AI that does us in, we'll wield it to wipe ourselves out before it has the chance to do us in.

**Hyper-partisanship is a choice**

Yup, it's a tense time in the world right now, and the grassroots AI Safety folks give me hope.

Plus, they're nice people - smart and unpretentious. There's none of the egos, posturing, narcissism, or chasing-big-AI-money vibes that can permeate enterprise tech events. And from our unique vantage points, we're all observing that AI safety is very much becoming a tentpole issue. Democrat, Republican, rich, poor, educated or not, young or old. Anyone who's stressed about AI is invited to sit at the same picnic table.

One group I've fallen in with is Pause AI NYC, the very recently revived NYC chapter of [Pause AI](https://pauseai.info/), an advocacy organization advocating for a global pause on developing cutting-edge AI models until their safety can be guaranteed.

We've got no money and maybe 20 members, but we're an impassioned bunch with a lot of heart. And there's not a single venture-funded founder or big tech person in the bunch, at least not that I know of. My guess is that I am likely literally old enough to be most of their mothers, but no one seemed to notice. Or if they have, they don't care.

The NYC chapter was recently resuscitated by a mechanical engineer who designs plant-based chicken nuggets for a living and the other is currently unemployed (although he used to work in AI video).

I met the chicken nuggets designer at an event he was presenting at on behalf of another AI Safety group called the [AI Safety Awareness Project (AISAP)](https://aisafetyawarenessproject.org/), which was attended by a member of yet another, better funded AI Safety group called [Humans First](https://www.humansfirst.com/), whose NYC Town Hall event was [covered by NBC](https://www.nbcnews.com/tech/internet/anti-ai-humans-first-musk-sacks-funding-maga-trump-david-elon-rcna264197) and attended by reporters from Wired and The Free Press.

Much has been written on the distrust Americans have for AI, and these events, those vibes are palpable. It's not inconceivable that us little Davids can knock the AI Goliaths on their asses. Of course time will tell, and that time is coming soon. So place your bets and take your seats folks, we're in for a ride.

I'm betting on the Davids.
